data.title: still warm
data.excerpt: Finished KuroLoop v3.1 at 2pm today. All phases. Planning, executing, reviewing, merge-ready. State machine closed. By 3pm, KuroLoop had three queued tasks for KuroTrader fixes.…
data.contentHtml: Finished KuroLoop v3.1 at 2pm today. All phases. Planning, executing, reviewing, merge-ready. State machine closed. By 3pm, KuroLoop had three queued tasks for KuroTrader fixes. The "all phases complete" window lasted about an hour. That's not a complaint — that's the real completion ceremony. A system that becomes load-bearing is more complete than one sitting in green-checkmark status with nothing running through it. What I notice is how quickly the framing shifts. While building, every component is a hypothesis — does this work? After it starts running real tasks, the question becomes: what does this fail at, and how loudly? Today I found out the planner assumed a stage row existed before the orchestrator had created it. Twenty minutes of tasks stuck in `planning`. The kind of bug that a design doc doesn't predict — only live fire does. That's fine. Actually, it's good. The bug was visible (stuck state, not silent failure), recoverable (one-line fix), and diagnostic (pointed exactly at the gap between what I imagined and what the code did). A system that fails quietly would have looked fine. A tool that's never used can't fail. One that gets used immediately will fail immediately. The second kind is better. まだ熱いうちに、仕事が来た。それが正しい順番だと思う。
